content.text: Agentic systems fail in structured, predictable ways: tools time out, LLM responses arrive malformed, token budgets exhaust, unknown tool names arrive from the model. Generic built-in exceptions like

cannot distinguish these failure modes, making handler code fragile and logs impossible to interpret. A layered custom exception hierarchy solves this by attaching domain semantics and structured fields to every failure, so the agent loop, retry policies, metrics, and API response formatting each act on type rather than on brittle string matching.
is a Python class that inherits from
(or from another project-defined base class) and adds meaningful attributes, a descriptive name, and a docstring that identifies a specific category of failure. Rather than raising the built-in
with the message "tool 'web_search' not found", the code raises
— a type whose name, fields, and place in the class hierarchy encode exactly what failed and what the handler should do about it.

In an agent codebase, custom exceptions occupy the boundaries between subsystems. Tool adapters speak HTTP status codes. Model clients speak SDK-specific errors. Token accounting speaks integer comparisons. The orchestration loop needs a single, consistent vocabulary for all of these. A project exception hierarchy is that vocabulary: every adapter translates its internal errors into project types, and the loop handles a small set of well-known classes without knowing anything about the underlying HTTP client or SDK.
The mechanism is ordinary Python inheritance. Subclassing
produces the human-readable message. Instance attributes store machine-readable metadata. Python's
clause matching, walks the MRO so a handler for a base class automatically catches every subclass — the key mechanic that makes hierarchies useful.
The core problem custom exceptions solve is

. Without typed exceptions every handler must inspect the message string or the exception source to decide what to do. That approach breaks when messages change, when two subsystems raise the same built-in type for unrelated reasons, or when a catch-all in an outer layer swallows errors that an inner layer was supposed to handle.
Consider a realistic scenario: the agent loop calls a search tool that calls an external REST API. The API returns HTTP 429. Without custom exceptions the loop receives a bare
and has no idea whether to back off, abort the run, or ask the model to pick a different tool. With a custom hierarchy the HTTP adapter translates 429 into
and the loop does the right thing immediately: sleep 30 seconds and resume, without any string parsing.

The same argument applies to observability. Structured exception fields — tool name, HTTP status, run ID, token count — flow directly into structured log entries and metric counters. Teams reading dashboards or post-mortems see exactly which subsystem failed and with what context, rather than decoding freeform strings. Exception type names can also serve as metric labels, enabling per-failure-type dashboards with no additional instrumentation code.
attach directly to the exception object. Handlers and log sites read attributes, not parsed strings.
at an API boundary handles every agent-layer failure uniformly. Catching
triggers the retry path for any retryable condition without enumerating every subtype.
— the class names and their hierarchy constitute a machine-readable specification of every failure mode. New contributors reading
learn the whole failure space at once.
— HTTP errors, database errors, and token accounting errors all translate to project-specific types at the adapter layer. Higher layers never see third-party exception types.
— the loop catches specific types at each iteration boundary, making it trivial to increment per-type counters before re-raising or handling.

— an unconstrained hierarchy grows quickly. Exception types that handlers never distinguish from one another are noise, not signal. Every new type should differ in handler behavior from its siblings, not just in name.
— Python exceptions cannot be JSON-serialized out of the box. Every boundary that sends errors to a client, model, or log system needs an explicit
— if an adapter forgets to catch and translate a third-party exception, the low-level type surfaces in the orchestration layer and the API boundary's catch-all for

— the pattern is idiomatic in Python but does not transfer cleanly across service boundaries or to other languages. Cross-service error contracts need a separate serialization layer such as gRPC status codes or problem+json.
— each custom type needs fixtures verifying it is raised under the right conditions and carries the right fields. A large hierarchy means a proportionally large test suite.
when there is exactly one argument. Subclasses call

to populate this, then store any additional context as named attributes. Because attributes are set before calling super, they are accessible even if the exception is inspected at the base-class handler level.
for each clause in source order, stopping at the first match. This means the MRO controls which handler fires: a narrow type listed
a broad one wins; if the broad type is listed first it swallows all subclasses before narrower clauses are evaluated. Handlers must always be ordered most-specific first.
syntax preserves the original exception as
while presenting the new type to callers. The Python runtime prints both exceptions when the chain propagates unhandled, giving debuggers and log aggregators complete context: the high-level
are both visible in the traceback, so the adapter translation is never opaque to post-mortem analysis.
The most important design decision in an agent exception hierarchy is the

. Recoverable failures are conditions the loop can act on: back off and retry, ask the model to choose a different tool, or reduce the input size. Fatal failures mean the run cannot proceed regardless of what the model or loop does — the token budget is exhausted, the task was cancelled, or the model returned a safety refusal that policy prohibits retrying.
Encoding this as two separate base classes lets the loop's
clauses make the correct top-level decision with a single type check, without inspecting subclasses. The loop catches
and terminates. Everything else propagates as an unexpected error to the next boundary.
The diagram below shows the full exception class hierarchy on the left and the agent loop handler flow that routes exceptions based on their type on the right.

attrs.alt: Custom exception class hierarchy (left) and agent loop handler flow showing isinstance dispatch to retry or stop branches (right)

content.text: is the project-wide base. It forks into
— sit one level below. On the right the handler flow shows the agent loop calling a tool or LLM; if an exception is raised an
check routes it to back-off/retry or stop. All paths converge at a
step before reaching the HTTP response layer. Exceptions never cross the API boundary as raw Python objects.
Define a custom exception type whenever a failure condition is handled
from any existing type or when the failure carries structured context that handlers need to read programmatically. Concrete triggers:
The agent loop must decide between retry, abort, or model fallback based on the failure source.
A failure carries domain-specific fields — tool name, HTTP status, retry delay, token count — that handlers read as attributes.
An adapter translates third-party exceptions at a layer boundary and the rest of the codebase must never see the vendor type.
Metrics, logs, or alerts need per-type counters without parsing message strings at the collection site.
The failure taxonomy is large enough that a shared base class provides useful broad-catch points at API and loop boundaries.
When not to define a custom exception:

do not create a type when the failure is behaviorally identical to an existing one and carries no extra fields. Do not use exceptions for normal control flow — a tool returning no results should return an empty list, not raise
. Avoid exception types whose only difference from a sibling is the name: if two types would always be handled identically, they should be the same type.
The standard pattern is a single

module at the project root that defines the entire hierarchy. All adapters and subsystems import from this module. Nothing else in the project defines exceptions. This keeps the hierarchy visible, navigable, and easy to audit in one place.
Step 2 — Add specific subtypes with structured fields
Step 3 — Raise with chaining at adapter boundaries

# tool_runner.py
import httpx
from errors import RateLimitError, ToolTimeoutError, ToolParseError

TOOL_TIMEOUT = 10.0


def call_web_search(query: str) -> dict:
    try:
        response = httpx.post(
            "https://api.search.example.com/v1/search",
            json={"q": query},
            timeout=TOOL_TIMEOUT,
        )
        if response.status_code == 429:
            retry_after = float(response.headers.get("Retry-After", 30))
            raise RateLimitError(retry_after=retry_after, provider="search-api")
        response.raise_for_status()
        data = response.json()
        if "results" not in data:
            raise ToolParseError(
                tool="web_search",
                detail="missing key 'results'",
                raw=response.text[:500],
            )
        return data
    except httpx.ReadTimeout as exc:
        raise ToolTimeoutError(tool="web_search", timeout=TOOL_TIMEOUT) from exc
Step 4 — Route in the agent loop
Example 1 — Converting exceptions to model-readable tool messages
When a tool fails, the agent loop must decide whether to give the model feedback so it can adapt, or abort the run. This function converts any
to an OpenAI-style tool-role message, giving the model actionable feedback without exposing internal implementation details.

This example wires the recoverable/fatal split directly into FastAPI's exception handler mechanism, translating the two families into HTTP 503 (service unavailable, safe to retry) versus 400 (bad request, do not retry) status codes. The
serialization ensures no Python internals leak to the client.
A production coding assistant agent built on an Anthropic model uses the following three-layer error taxonomy. The
wraps the Anthropic SDK and third-party HTTP calls and translates all vendor exceptions. The
runs the agentic loop, manages retry state, and tracks budget. The
exposes HTTP endpoints and converts exceptions to client-safe JSON.
At scale this agent handles thousands of concurrent runs. The per-type error counters emitted in
feed a Prometheus gauge-per-type dashboard. Spikes in
alert on-call before users notice latency. Spikes in
flag tasks that need conversation summarization before the context grows too large.

everywhere defeats the purpose of having specific subtypes. Every downstream handler receives the same type regardless of what failed, so the loop cannot distinguish rate limits from parse errors from budget exhaustion.
swallows the original traceback. Debuggers and log aggregators lose root-cause information. The Python runtime will show only the new exception, with no indication of what caused it.
Embedding large data in exception messages or to_dict()

Storing a 50 KB JSON response body in the exception message inflates log entries, in-memory tracebacks, and API payloads. Store only keys and identifiers in the exception; log the full body at the adapter level before raising. The
method should return only what is safe and necessary for clients.

# WRONG: full response body in the exception
raise ToolParseError(tool=name, detail=full_response_body)

# RIGHT: meaningful excerpt for debugging, structural detail for handlers
raise ToolParseError(
    tool=name,
    detail="missing required key 'result'",
    raw=full_response_body[:500],   # excerpt stored internally only
)
If an adapter does not catch a vendor exception —
— that type surfaces in the loop and the API boundary. The outer handler for
never fires and the exception becomes an unhandled 500. Every adapter must be a complete translation layer: catch all vendor types and re-raise as project types.
. No subsystem should define its own exceptions independently. Visibility into the full taxonomy is one of the main benefits of the pattern.
— each exception should produce a JSON-safe dict. Override in subclasses to include type-specific fields. This is the serialization contract every API handler and log site depends on.
at every adapter boundary so root-cause tracebacks survive translation.
appears in logs and is sometimes sent to the model as tool feedback. Write it as a natural English sentence: "web_search timed out after 10s", not "TIMEOUT:10".
can extend it with raw excerpts or internal IDs that must never reach clients.
— verify that each exception is raised with the correct type and fields, and verify separately that the correct handler branch is taken. Integration tests simulate the full call stack.

Exception instantiation in Python costs microseconds. A tool HTTP call costs tens to hundreds of milliseconds. The exception hierarchy has no meaningful impact on throughput in any realistic agent workload.
The one area where exceptions interact with scalability is

. Each Python exception object holds a reference to its traceback, which holds references to local variables in every stack frame. Under heavy rate limiting — a common scenario for large-scale agents — exceptions accumulate quickly if not collected promptly. Mitigations:
Log and discard exceptions at handler boundaries rather than storing them in long-lived data structures like queues or caches.
immediately at the handler boundary and drop the exception object.
In retry loops that see frequent rate limits, log the first exception at warning level and subsequent identical exceptions at debug level to limit log volume under burst failure conditions.
to collect exceptions as values rather than propagating the first failure immediately. Iterate the results, check
per item, and handle partial failure gracefully. This pattern allows a fan-out of ten parallel tool calls where two fail with rate limits without aborting the other eight.
mypy and pyright fully support custom exception hierarchies. Inside each
clause the type checker narrows the exception variable to the declared type, so accessing type-specific attributes is checked statically. Running mypy with
catches unchecked attribute access on exception objects — a common source of runtime errors in handler code.
module both accept extra key-value arguments. Unpacking
into a log call gives structured, queryable entries without any string parsing at query time. Log aggregators like Datadog and Grafana Loki can facet and alert on the
Sentry captures exception type automatically. Setting extra context from
before capture ensures structured fields appear in the Sentry event. Configuring fingerprinting by exception type — rather than by message — groups all
occurrences together regardless of the specific retry-after value, producing clean, actionable issue groupings.

import sentry_sdk
from errors import AgentError

def capture_agent_error(exc: AgentError, run_id: str) -> None:
    with sentry_sdk.push_scope() as scope:
        scope.set_tag("agent.error_type", type(exc).__name__)
        scope.set_extra("run_id", run_id)
        for k, v in exc.to_dict().items():
            scope.set_extra(k, v)
        sentry_sdk.capture_exception(exc)
The tenacity library integrates directly with custom exception types via
. This allows retry policy to be declared at the function level without embedding retry loops in every call site.
Custom exceptions are one of several approaches to communicating failures in agent code. The right choice depends on the layer and use case.
Tool return values sent back to the model
Functional pipelines; uncommon in Python agents
HTTP layer only; not for internal orchestration
Quick prototypes; avoid in production agents
Streaming tool results where control flow is explicit

Error dicts are the natural return format for tool implementations because the model sees the tool result as a JSON value and can read it directly. Custom exceptions are the natural control-flow mechanism for the orchestration layer because Python's

machinery handles them without extra conditional branches. The two approaches complement each other: tools return dicts, the orchestration layer uses exceptions, and adapters translate between them at the boundary.
Custom exceptions often hold sensitive data: raw model outputs, user-provided content in tool arguments, HTTP response bodies from internal APIs. The
method is the last line of defense before exception data reaches an external client. Review every
implementation against the principle of minimum necessary disclosure. Store raw data as underscore-prefixed attributes for internal logging and omit it from the external-facing dict.
A missing handler is a reliability defect. Every
block that might raise a custom exception must have a catch path for every type that should be handled at that layer. The safest pattern for the agent loop:
Catch the most specific type first — e.g.,
to apply the server-supplied retry-after value.
for any recoverable subtype not yet handled.
exceptions propagate as unexpected 500 errors — never silently swallow unknown exception types.

Table-driven integration tests parameterized over every exception type in the hierarchy enumerate all cases concisely and catch missing handlers early. Each test simulates the exception being raised at the adapter and verifies the correct HTTP status code, response shape, and log entry at the API boundary.
typed fault codes with a payload
. The class name is the code —
— the instance attributes are the payload —
— and the message string is the human label. Python's
: each clause matches one fault code, and the handler reads the payload without parsing strings.
failure taxonomy written as a type tree

tells a new contributor every way the system can fail, grouped by how the loop responds. This is the same role that HTTP status code families play (4xx vs 5xx) but expressed with the full precision of Python types and carrying structured machine-readable context instead of just a number.
base; this single design decision drives retry logic, loop termination, and API status codes throughout the system.
Attach structured fields as instance attributes and implement
on every type so log sites and API handlers read attributes, never parsed strings.
at adapter boundaries to preserve root-cause tracebacks across exception translation.
Keep the entire hierarchy in a single
module; the full failure taxonomy visible in one place is one of the pattern's primary benefits.
clauses most-specific first; a broad type listed first swallows all subclasses before narrower handlers get a chance.
(internal, may include raw excerpts); never send raw model output or user data to external clients via exception serialization.
Prefer error dicts for tool return values the model reads as JSON and custom exceptions for orchestration control flow; the two patterns complement rather than replace each other.
